perturbation.original_text: Horst Entertainment shall send this amount to Galacticomm by no later than Wednesday for accesses used from the previuos week (Monday thru Sunday).
perturbation.changed_text: Horst Entertainment shall send this amount to Galacticomm by no later than 60 days after the end of the month in which the accesses occurred.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline from Wednesday of the following week to 60 days after the end of the month introduces a significant delay in payment that could violate state prompt payment laws. Many states have laws requiring more prompt payment than this.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 215.422 concerns prompt payment requirements by state agencies, implying a standard of reasonable and timely payment that a 60-day delay could potentially contradict, especially if standard industry practices dictate faster payment terms. While this statute applies to state agencies, it sets a general expectation for prompt payment that could be relevant in interpreting the reasonableness of payment terms in other contracts under Florida law.
perturbation.justification: The change from a payment deadline of Wednesday of the following week to 60 days after the end of the month introduces a substantial delay. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that such a delay could violate state prompt payment laws, and the Law Explanation mentions Florida Statute 215.422 which concerns prompt payment requirements. The inconsistency in payment deadlines directly contradicts the principle of 'prompt payment' implied by the statute and mentioned in the Law Explanation, thus creating a legal contradiction as the changed text now violates a standard that prioritizes timely payments.

perturbation.original_text: Galacticomm must provide a person(s) to correct any technical problems (Server being down or inaccessible) 24 hours per day, 7 days per week.
perturbation.changed_text: Galacticomm will attempt to correct any technical problems (Server being down or inaccessible) during normal business hours, Monday to Friday, excluding holidays.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the technical support availability from 24/7 to standard business hours reduces the level of service. This could contradict reasonable expectations and potentially violate implied warranties if the service interruption significantly impacts the client's business operations, and reasonable uptime is expected.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-314 establishes the implied warranty of merchantability, which requires that goods or services sold are fit for the ordinary purpose for which they are used. By reducing technical support availability, the modified text could be interpreted as a breach of this warranty if the web hosting service becomes unreliable during off-hours, thus not fulfilling its ordinary purpose of providing continuous web hosting.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification from 24/7 support to business hours directly contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability under UCC § 2-314. The original text ensures continuous service, aligning with the 'ordinary purpose' of web hosting, while the changed text limits support, potentially rendering the service unreliable during off-hours, as the Law Explanation describes. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that goods must be 'fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used', and limiting technical support in this way would violate that.

perturbation.original_text: Any disputes arising herein shall be settled in a court in FLorida.
perturbation.changed_text: Any disputes arising herein shall be settled by binding arbitration in a location of Galacticomm's choosing, with no right to appeal.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing binding arbitration with no right to appeal, and allowing Galacticomm to choose the location, can be challenged. Many states and federal laws ensure fairness in dispute resolution processes and protect rights to appeal certain arbitration outcomes, especially regarding fairness and impartiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: 9 U.S. Code § 10 outlines the conditions under which an arbitration award can be vacated, including instances of corruption, fraud, or evident partiality of the arbitrator. The modified text, which denies any right to appeal, attempts to bypass these statutory provisions, potentially rendering the arbitration clause unenforceable under the FAA if the arbitration process is unfair or biased.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to

Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672; Pub. L. 101–552, §5, Nov. 15, 1990, 104 Stat. 2745; Pub. L. 102–354, §5(b)(4), Aug. 26, 1992, 106 Stat. 946; Pub. L. 107–169, §1, May 7, 2002, 116 Stat. 132.) (1) to (4), and substituted a semicolon for period at end in pars. (1
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates binding arbitration with no right to appeal and allows Galacticomm to choose the location, which directly contradicts the legal provisions outlined in 9 U.S. Code § 10. This section specifies grounds for vacating an arbitration award, including corruption, fraud, partiality of the arbitrator, misconduct, or exceeding powers. By denying any right to appeal, the modified text attempts to bypass these statutory provisions, potentially rendering the arbitration clause unenforceable under the FAA if the arbitration process is unfair or biased, as it eliminates the possibility of judicial review to ensure fairness and compliance with the law. The scraped law snippets explicitly state the conditions under which a court may vacate an arbitration award, emphasizing the importance of impartiality and due process, which the changed text undermines.
